# Copilot Instructions

## Build, Test, and Lint

The workspace uses **Rust edition 2024**. Be aware of its implications: `gen` is a reserved keyword, `unsafe` blocks are required inside `unsafe fn` bodies, and lifetime capture rules in `impl Trait` return types have changed.

```sh
# Build the full workspace
cargo build --workspace

# Run all tests (network-dependent tests are #[ignore] by default)
cargo test --workspace

# Run a single test by name
cargo test -p c-ares -- test_name
cargo test -p c-ares-resolver -- test_name

# Run ignored network tests (requires network access)
cargo test --workspace -- --ignored

# Lint
cargo clippy --workspace --tests --examples -- -D warnings

# Format check
cargo fmt --all -- --check
```

Prefer `--features vendored` when running tests, examples, and clippy to ensure you build against the latest vendored c-ares and get full coverage of version-gated APIs:

```sh
cargo test --workspace --features vendored
cargo clippy --workspace --tests --examples --features vendored -- -D warnings
cargo run -p c-ares-resolver --features vendored --example futures
```

Checkout must include submodules (`git clone --recurse-submodules` or `git submodule update --init`) for vendored builds.

### Common gotchas

- **`#![deny(missing_docs)]`** — The `c-ares` and `c-ares-resolver` crates enforce this. Every public item (type, method, variant, field) must have a doc comment or the build will fail. (`c-ares-sys` does not enforce this, as its public API is generated.)
- **Build scripts share version-detection logic** — `c-ares/build.rs` and `c-ares-resolver/build.rs` both emit `cfg(cares1_XX)` flags. Each only needs gates for versions it actually uses; they need not be identical.
- **CI does not use `--features vendored`** — CI tests against the system `libcares`, so version-gated code may not be exercised there. Always test locally with `--features vendored`.
- **Shared workspace dependencies** — `c-types` and `itertools` are declared in the root `Cargo.toml` under `[workspace.dependencies]` and referenced with `workspace = true` in each crate's `Cargo.toml`.

## Architecture

This is a Rust workspace with three crates forming a layered FFI wrapper around the [c-ares](https://c-ares.org/) C library:

- **`c-ares-sys`** — Raw unsafe FFI bindings. Bindings in `src/ffi.rs` and `src/constants.rs` are generated by `bindgen` via `generate-ffi.sh` and checked in (not generated at build time) — do not hand-edit these files. The build script lives at `build/main.rs` (not `build.rs`) and handles probing for an installed `libcares` or building the vendored submodule at `c-ares-sys/c-ares`.
- **`c-ares`** — Safe wrapper. Provides `Channel` (the core event-loop-driven resolver), `Options` (builder for channel config), and per-record-type result wrappers (`AResults`, `MXResults`, etc.). You must drive the event loop yourself at this layer.
- **`c-ares-resolver`** — High-level resolver. Wraps `c-ares::Channel` in an internal event-loop thread and exposes `FutureResolver`, `BlockingResolver`, and callback-based `Resolver`.

### Key files

When navigating the codebase, these are the most important files to know about:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `c-ares/src/macros.rs` | `ares_call!`, `ares_query!`, `ares_search!`, `ares_callback!` — the macros that implement the callback boxing/FFI/reclamation lifecycle |
| `c-ares/src/panic.rs` | `panic::catch` / `panic::propagate` — safely round-trips Rust panics across FFI boundaries |
| `c-ares/src/utils.rs` | String/pointer conversion helpers, IP address conversions, `status_to_result`, `version()` |
| `c-ares/src/types.rs` | `QueryType`, `DnsClass`, `AddressFamily`, `Socket` enums |
| `c-ares/src/error.rs` | `Error` enum (maps `ares_status_t`), `Result<T>` type alias |
| `c-ares/src/channel.rs` | `Channel` — all query/search methods live here |
| `c-ares/src/dns/` | Structured DNS record API (generic message parsing/writing, gated behind `cares1_28`) |
| `c-ares/build.rs` | Version detection — emits `cfg(cares1_XX)` flags |
| `c-ares-resolver/src/error.rs` | `Error` enum wrapping both `io::Error` and `c_ares::Error` |
| `c-ares-resolver/src/eventloop.rs` | Background event-loop thread, socket management via `polling::Poller` |
| `c-ares-resolver/build.rs` | Version detection — emits the subset of `cfg(cares1_XX)` flags used by this crate |
| `c-ares-resolver/src/host.rs` | Example of an owned resolver result type with `From<c_ares::HostResults>` |
| `c-ares-resolver/src/futureresolver.rs` | `FutureResolver` and the `futurize!` macro |
| `c-ares-resolver/src/blockingresolver.rs` | `BlockingResolver` and the `blockify!` macro |
| `c-ares-sys/build/main.rs` | Vendored C build logic (autotools/cmake/nmake) |

### Feature flags and c-ares version

Which c-ares version you link against depends on your environment and feature flags. Without `vendored`, the build uses whatever system `libcares` is installed — which may be old and lack newer APIs. With `vendored`, the build uses the c-ares source pinned in the git submodule, which is typically the latest release.

All three crates share the same feature flag structure:

- `maybe-vendored` (default) — use system `libcares` if found, fall back to vendored build.
- `vendored` — always build vendored `c-ares` from the git submodule. Prefer this for development to ensure full API coverage.
- `build-cmake` — use CMake instead of autotools for the vendored build (faster on Unix).

### Version-gated APIs

Build scripts detect the `c-ares` version and emit `cfg` flags like `cares1_29`, `cares1_34`, etc. Code gated behind these flags is only compiled when linked against a sufficiently new `c-ares`.

The currently emitted version cfgs are: `cares1_19`, `cares1_20`, `cares1_22`, `cares1_23`, `cares1_24`, `cares1_27`, `cares1_28`, `cares1_29`, `cares1_30`, `cares1_34`.

To add a new version gate:
1. Determine the c-ares version that introduced the API. c-ares encodes versions as `(major << 16) | (minor << 8) | patch`, so e.g. 1.35.0 = `0x1_23_00`.
2. Add the gate to the build script of each crate that uses it (`c-ares/build.rs` and/or `c-ares-resolver/build.rs`):
   ```rust
   println!("cargo::rustc-check-cfg=cfg(cares1_35)");
   if version >= 0x1_23_00 {
       println!("cargo:rustc-cfg=cares1_35");
   }
   ```
   The `rustc-check-cfg` line must come before or alongside the conditional `rustc-cfg` line.
3. Gate your new code with `#[cfg(cares1_35)]`.

## Conventions

### Error handling

`c_ares::Error` maps `ares_status_t` variants; `c_ares::Result<T>` is an alias for `result::Result<T, Error>`. The bridge between C status codes and Rust errors is `status_to_result` in `utils.rs`. `c_ares_resolver::Error` wraps both `io::Error` and `c_ares::Error`.

### DNS record wrapper pattern

Each DNS record type (`a.rs`, `aaaa.rs`, `mx.rs`, etc. in `c-ares/src/`) follows a consistent triple:

- `XResults` — owned collection holding a raw pointer to the C reply struct. Frees C memory in `Drop` via `ares_free_data()`. Implements `Display`, `Send`, `Sync`.
- `XResult<'a>` — borrowed view into a single record, lifetime-bound to the owning `XResults`. Provides accessor methods returning borrowed data. Implements `Display`, `Send`, `Sync`.
- `XResultsIter<'a>` — iterator that walks the C linked list, yielding `XResult<'a>` items. Implements `Send`, `Sync`.

Each record module also exports a `pub(crate) unsafe extern "C" fn query_x_callback<F>` that bridges the C callback into the typed Rust closure using the `ares_callback!` macro.

### Callback lifecycle and macros

The callback system is built on four macros in `c-ares/src/macros.rs`:

- **`ares_call!`** — The foundation. Converts the handler name to a `CString`, boxes the closure with `Box::into_raw`, calls the specified `c_ares_sys` function, then calls `panic::propagate()` to re-raise any panic caught during a prior callback.
- **`ares_query!`** / **`ares_search!`** — Shorthands for `ares_call!` with `ares_query` / `ares_search`.
- **`ares_callback!`** — Used inside the `unsafe extern "C"` callback function. Checks the status, parses the response buffer on success, reclaims the boxed closure with `Box::from_raw`, and invokes it inside `panic::catch`.

The full lifecycle is:
1. Caller boxes the closure → `Box::into_raw` → raw pointer passed to C (via `ares_call!`).
2. C library invokes the callback with the raw pointer.
3. Callback reclaims the box → `Box::from_raw` → calls the closure (via `ares_callback!`).
4. `panic::catch` wraps the handler call; if it panics, the panic is stored in a thread-local.
5. Back in the caller, `panic::propagate()` resumes any stored panic on the Rust side.

When adding a new query method, use `ares_query!`/`ares_search!` on the caller side and `ares_callback!` in the callback — do not hand-write the boxing/unboxing.

### Resolver patterns

The `c-ares-resolver` crate wraps `c-ares::Channel` in three resolver styles:

- **`Resolver`** — callback-based. Methods take a closure parameter and forward to `self.ares_channel.lock().unwrap().query_x(name, handler)`.
- **`FutureResolver`** — async. Methods use the `futurize!` macro: creates a oneshot channel, calls the inner `Resolver` method with a callback that sends the result, returns a `CAresFuture`.
- **`BlockingResolver`** — synchronous. Methods use the `blockify!` macro: creates a `sync_channel(1)`, calls the inner `Resolver`, blocks on `recv()`.

For most DNS record types, the resolver methods return `c_ares` types directly. Only `HostResults` and `NameInfoResult` have resolver-local owned types with `From` impls that copy data out of borrowed C memory.

### FFI safety

- Owned wrappers (`DnsRecord`, `AresString`, `AresBuf`, `HostentOwned`) free C memory in their `Drop` impl.
- Borrowed views (`DnsRr`, `HostentBorrowed<'a>`) never free memory and are lifetime-bound to their parent.
- All raw-pointer-based result types have explicit `unsafe impl Send` and `unsafe impl Sync`. These are safe because each wrapper exclusively owns its C allocation with no shared mutable state.

### Platform-specific code

Some code is platform-gated. Examples and tests using `epoll` or `nix` are gated behind `#[cfg(all(unix, any(target_os = "linux", target_os = "android")))]`. Socket address conversions in `utils.rs` have BSD/macOS/Windows variants. When adding platform-specific code, follow the existing pattern of providing a fallback or `println!("not supported")` stub on other platforms.

### Testing

- Network-dependent tests are marked `#[ignore = "requires network"]` and query public DNS (e.g., `google.com` via `8.8.8.8`). They do not run in CI by default.
- Test helpers live in `tests/common/mod.rs` in each crate. `c-ares` tests use `process_channel()` to drive the event loop manually.
- Unit tests in each record module should check `Send`/`Sync` bounds and invalid-data parsing.
- Integration tests for each record type should exist in `c-ares/tests/query.rs` and in all three resolver test suites (`callback_resolver.rs`, `future_resolver.rs`, `blocking_resolver.rs`).
